turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court in case 22-500, Great Lakes Insurance versus Raiders Retreat Realty.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: This is a Maritime Law case, in particular a case about the enforceability of choice-of-law provisions in maritime contracts. To insure its boat, Raiders Retreat Realty, a Pennsylvania business, purchased a policy from Great Lakes Insurance, a company organized in Germany and headquartered in the United Kingdom. The insurance contract included a choice-of-law provision that as relevant here, selected New York law to govern future disputes between the parties. Raiders’ boat suffered an accident in Florida and Great Lakes and Raiders ended up in litigation in Pennsylvania over whether Great Lakes was required to provide insurance coverage. The question before this Court is whether the contract's choice-of-law provision selecting New York law should govern this dispute rather than Pennsylvania law where the suit was filed or another state with a greater interest in the dispute. We conclude that the choice-of-law provision selecting New York law should govern. Article III of the Constitution extends the Federal Judicial power to all cases of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction. Under the Constitution Federal Courts make decisional law for maritime cases. When a Federal Court decides a maritime case it acts as a federal common law court, much as state courts do in state common law cases. As relevant here longstanding precedent establishes the Federal Maritime rule that choice-of-law provisions in maritime contracts are presumptively enforceable. Although no recent case of this Court has addressed the issue, the Court has traditionally enforced choice-of-law provisions in maritime contracts. The Court’s traditional enforcement of choice-of-law provisions corresponds to the Court’s precedents in the analogous forum-selection context. The Court has pronounced that forum-selection clauses in maritime contracts are presumptively valid under federal maritime law and should be enforced unless doing so would be unreasonable under the circumstances. We hold that the same basic rule should apply in the choice-of-law context. Choice-of-law provision serve important purposes in maritime commerce. For example, identifying the governing law in advance choice-of-law provisions allow parties to avoid later disputes as well as ensuing litigation and its attendant costs. Raiders says however that federal maritime law should not enforce choice-of-law provisions in cases where enforcing the law of the state designated by the contract would contravene the fundamental public policy of the state with the greatest interest in the dispute. We disagree with that argument. A federal presumption of enforceability would not be much of a presumption if it could be routinely swept aside based on 50 states public policy determinations. In sum, choice-of-law provisions in maritime contracts are presumptively enforceable as a matter of federal maritime law, with certain narrow exceptions, and no exception to the presumption applies in this case. We therefore reverse the judgment of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit. The opinion of the Court is unanimous. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion.
